title: Introduction
text: Corvid is the memory-management-unit and translation-lookaside-buffer subsystem built into every core of Talvera Systems' Sawtooth processor family, first taped out in 2018 and now in its second major hardware revision. Rather than treating address translation as a single flat cache, Corvid splits the job across a small, fast first-level TLB tuned for single-cycle lookup and a larger, slower second-level TLB that backs it up when a first-level lookup misses. Software running on a Sawtooth core never issues an explicit translation request; every load, store, and instruction fetch passes through Corvid transparently, and a miss at every level triggers a hardware page-table walk rather than a trap into supervisor software. This hardware-walked design was a deliberate choice: earlier Talvera cores used software-managed translation and paid a substantial instruction-count penalty on every miss, a cost Corvid was built specifically to eliminate.

title: TLB Structure
text: Each Sawtooth core's first-level data TLB, the L1 DTLB, holds 64 entries organized 4-way set associative, while the first-level instruction TLB, the L1 ITLB, holds 48 entries fully associative. Both first-level structures return a translation in a single cycle on a hit, which occurs on roughly 98.6 percent of accesses under the SPECint-derived workload suite Talvera uses for internal characterization. Behind them sits a unified second-level TLB, the L2 TLB, with 2,048 entries arranged 8-way set associative and shared by the two hardware threads on a single core. The L2 TLB adds a fixed 6-cycle lookup latency on top of the L1 miss penalty. Measured across a 32-core Sawtooth R3 die, aggregate L2 TLB miss rate stayed below 0.4 percent during typical database workloads.

title: Page-Table Walk Mechanism
text: When both TLB levels miss, Corvid's hardware page-table walker takes over, resolving the address through a four-level radix page table matching the layout used by Talvera's existing operating-system port. The walker reads one table entry per level, using the faulting virtual address's index bits to select the entry at each step, and installs the resulting translation into both TLB levels once the walk completes. Rather than issuing four independent memory reads for every miss, the walker checks a small walk cache first, which stores intermediate table-entry results from earlier walks and lets a new walk skip levels whose entry it already has cached. A walk that hits the walk cache at every intermediate level still needs one final memory read to fetch the leaf entry, but avoids the three upper-level reads a cold walk requires. This walk cache is the single largest contributor to Corvid's lower average miss penalty compared to Talvera's earlier, walk-cache-free MMU generation.

title: Page-Walk Latency
text: A cold four-level page-table walk on Sawtooth R3 costs 42 cycles when every intermediate level misses the walk cache, measured at a 3.4 GHz core clock with single-channel DDR5-4800 memory. The walk cache itself holds 512 entries per core and reaches a steady-state hit rate of 71 percent under mixed workloads, cutting the effective walk latency to roughly 18 cycles on average. A walk that hits the walk cache at the top three levels but misses only the leaf entry completes in 11 cycles. Talvera's internal telemetry from the R3 validation suite recorded a 99.7 percent walk-completion rate within 60 cycles across 40 million sampled walks, with the remaining 0.3 percent delayed by concurrent memory-controller queuing.

title: Miss Penalty by Page Size
text:
Miss penalty varies by page size because larger pages reduce the number of table levels Corvid's walker must traverse before reaching a leaf entry, as summarized below.

| Page size | L1 TLB miss penalty | L2 TLB miss penalty | Full walk penalty |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 KB | 6 cycles | 18 cycles | 42 cycles |
| 2 MB | 6 cycles | 14 cycles | 31 cycles |
| 1 GB | 6 cycles | 9 cycles | 19 cycles |

The full-walk column drops sharply for 1 GB pages because the walker can stop after resolving the second table level instead of all four, skipping two levels of memory reads entirely and avoiding the associated round trips to main memory.

title: Address-Space Identifiers
text: Every entry Corvid installs into either TLB level is tagged with a 16-bit address-space identifier, or ASID, matching the process whose page table produced the translation. This tagging lets translations belonging to a process that is not currently running remain valid in the TLB rather than being flushed the moment the scheduler switches to a different process. On a context switch, the operating system loads the new process's ASID into a dedicated control register, and Corvid simply ignores any TLB entry whose stored ASID does not match the active one, without deleting it. A process that is scheduled back onto the same core shortly afterward can therefore still hit on translations left over from its previous time slice. This behavior matters most on workloads with frequent, short-lived context switches, where a flush-on-switch design would otherwise force every process to rebuild its working set of translations from scratch on every single scheduling quantum.

title: ASID Allocation
text: Corvid's 16-bit ASID field supports 65,536 live identifiers per core cluster before recycling becomes necessary. Talvera's reference kernel port reserves ASID 0 for the hypervisor and allocates the remaining 65,535 values to guest processes on a rotating basis. Under a sustained workload of 4,000 concurrent short-lived processes, measured on an eight-core Sawtooth R3 cluster, the kernel recycles the full ASID space roughly every 260 milliseconds, forcing a targeted TLB invalidation only for entries carrying the specific recycled ASID rather than a full flush. That targeted invalidation touches, on average, 3.1 TLB entries per recycling event across both TLB levels combined, a cost the reference kernel's telemetry logs separately from ordinary miss statistics.

title: TLB Shootdown Procedure
text:
When one core unmaps or changes permissions on a page that other cores may have cached translations for, Corvid requires the initiating core to run the following shootdown sequence before the unmap is considered complete. 1. Invalidate the local core's own TLB entries for the affected virtual address range.
2. Send a targeted inter-processor interrupt to every core listed as having potentially touched the affected address space.
3. Each receiving core invalidates its matching TLB entries and acknowledges completion.
4. The initiating core blocks further modification of the affected page table until every acknowledgment arrives.
5. Once all acknowledgments are collected, the initiating core releases the page-table lock. On a densely shared mapping touching many cores, step two's interrupt fan-out is consistently the slowest part of the sequence.

title: Miss-Penalty Analysis
text: Corvid's average memory access time follows the standard AMAT decomposition adapted to two TLB levels: AMAT = t_L1 + m_L1 * (t_L2 + m_L2 * t_walk), where t_L1 and t_L2 are the fixed lookup latencies of the two TLB levels, m_L1 and m_L2 are their respective miss rates, and t_walk is the average page-table-walk penalty. Substituting Sawtooth R3's measured values, t_L1 = 1 cycle, m_L1 = 0.014, t_L2 = 6 cycles, m_L2 = 0.004, and t_walk = 18 cycles, yields AMAT roughly 1.08 cycles, dominated overwhelmingly by the 98.6 percent L1 hit rate rather than by the comparatively rare walk penalty. This formula is why Talvera's design reviews prioritize shrinking m_L1, even by a fraction of a percent, over further reducing t_walk, since m_L1 sits outside both product terms and scales the entire miss-penalty contribution.

title: Prior Research: Walk-Cache Design
text: Hardware-managed page-table walk caching predates Corvid by nearly a decade, and much of the foundational analysis comes from research published outside English-language venues. Kim and Park's 2013 study, 다단계 페이지 테이블 워크 캐시의 설계와 성능 분석 (Design and Performance Analysis of Multi-Level Page-Table Walk Caches), measured walk-cache hit-rate sensitivity to associativity across several radix-tree depths, a methodology Talvera's Corvid architects adapted directly when sizing the 512-entry walk cache described elsewhere in this specification. The paper's central finding, that walk-cache benefit diminishes sharply past four cached intermediate levels, informed Talvera's decision to cap Corvid's walk cache at exactly the four levels its own page-table format actually uses, rather than over-provisioning for deeper hierarchies the hardware will never encounter.

title: Huge Page Support
text: Corvid supports three page sizes concurrently within a single process's address space: the standard 4 KB base page, a 2 MB huge page, and a 1 GB gigantic page, each represented by a leaf entry at a different level of the same four-level radix page table. Software requests a larger page size by setting a size hint in the relevant page-table entry rather than through a separate translation path, so Corvid's walker and both TLB levels treat all three sizes through the same lookup logic. Because a single TLB entry for a 2 MB or 1 GB page covers far more address space than a 4 KB entry, workloads with large, contiguous memory footprints, in-memory databases and scientific computing kernels in particular, see a substantial drop in TLB miss rate simply by remapping their largest allocations onto huge pages, without any change to application code beyond the initial allocation request.

title: Huge-Page TLB Reach
text: A full 64-entry L1 DTLB covers 256 KB of address space when populated entirely with 4 KB pages, but the same 64 entries cover 128 MB when populated with 2 MB pages, and 64 GB when populated with 1 GB pages. Talvera's internal benchmarking on a memory-resident analytics workload with a 48 GB working set showed L2 TLB miss rate falling from 3.8 percent under 4 KB pages to 0.06 percent after remapping the workload's primary heap onto 1 GB pages. The same test recorded a 21 percent reduction in end-to-end query latency, attributed almost entirely to the drop in page-walk frequency rather than to any change in the underlying query engine.

title: TLB Reach by Configuration
text:
Effective TLB reach scales directly with page size for a fixed entry count, as the following comparison across Corvid's two TLB levels shows.

| Configuration | Entries | Page size | Effective reach |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 DTLB | 64 | 4 KB | 256 KB |
| L1 DTLB | 64 | 2 MB | 128 MB |
| L2 TLB | 2048 | 4 KB | 8 MB |
| L2 TLB | 2048 | 1 GB | 2 TB |

The L2 TLB's reach under 1 GB pages exceeds the physical memory capacity of most single-socket Sawtooth deployments, meaning huge-page-mapped working sets rarely exhaust it in practice, though smaller 4 KB-mapped workloads still exhaust the L2 TLB routinely.

title: FAQ: L1 DTLB Sizing
text: Q: How large is the L1 DTLB on a Sawtooth core, and why doesn't Talvera just make it bigger? A: Each Sawtooth core's first-level data TLB, the L1 DTLB, holds 128 entries organized 4-way set associative, while the first-level instruction TLB, the L1 ITLB, holds 48 entries fully associative. Growing it further would push single-cycle lookup out of reach at Sawtooth's target clock frequency, since larger associative structures need more comparator logic per access, and that extra logic adds gate delay that no longer fits inside one cycle at 3.4 GHz. Talvera's architects have evaluated a 96-entry variant internally but found the marginal miss-rate improvement did not justify the added critical-path risk, and no such variant has shipped.

title: Shootdown Interrupt Cost
text: The wall-clock cost of a TLB shootdown scales with the number of cores holding a copy of the affected translation, following Cost = t_ipi + n * t_ack, where t_ipi is the fixed latency to dispatch the initial inter-processor interrupt broadcast, n is the number of remote cores that must acknowledge, and t_ack is the average per-core acknowledgment latency including invalidation and interrupt-return overhead. On a 32-core Sawtooth R3 die, t_ipi measures approximately 210 nanoseconds and t_ack averages 340 nanoseconds per core, so a shootdown touching all 31 remote cores costs roughly 10.7 microseconds end to end. Because the n * t_ack term dominates for any shootdown touching more than a handful of cores, Talvera's scheduler deliberately narrows the shootdown target list to only cores known to have actually accessed the affected address range, rather than broadcasting to every core unconditionally.

title: Walk-Cache Reconfiguration Checklist
text:
Field engineers adjusting Corvid's walk-cache parameters for a custom Sawtooth deployment follow this validation sequence before deploying a modified configuration to production silicon. 1. Confirm the target workload's working-set size and typical page-table depth using the profiling counters exposed by Corvid's performance-monitoring unit.
2. Simulate the proposed walk-cache entry count against captured traces using Talvera's offline TLB simulator.
3. Verify the simulated hit rate improves over the current configuration by at least 2 percentage points before proceeding.
4. Apply the new configuration to a single core in a staging cluster and monitor for at least 72 hours.
5. Roll the configuration out cluster-wide only after staging shows no regression in tail walk latency. Skipping step three has historically been the most common cause of reconfiguration attempts that Talvera later had to roll back.

title: Prior Research: ASID Recycling
text: ASID-recycling strategy also draws on published work outside Talvera's own research group. Brandt's 2015 paper, Wiederverwendung von Adressraum-Identifikatoren in Mehrkernprozessoren (Recycling Address-Space Identifiers in Multicore Processors), analyzed the tradeoff between ASID field width and the frequency of forced targeted invalidations under heavy process-creation workloads, concluding that a 16-bit field strikes a reasonable balance for server-class core counts without consuming excessive TLB tag bits. Talvera's Corvid architects cite this analysis directly when justifying the 16-bit ASID width described in this specification's allocation section, noting that Brandt's simulated recycling-interval data closely matches Talvera's own measured 260-millisecond recycling interval under comparable process-creation load, despite the decade-long gap between the two studies.

title: Generational Benchmark Comparison
text: Corvid's second hardware generation, shipping in Sawtooth R4 since January 2024, reduced average L2 TLB miss rate from 0.61 percent on R3 to 0.34 percent on R4, measured across Talvera's standard database and analytics benchmark suite. The walk cache grew from 512 to 768 entries per core between generations, while cold walk latency dropped from 42 to 37 cycles thanks to a wider memory-controller interface feeding the walker. Aggregate shootdown interrupt latency, t_ipi, improved only marginally, from 210 to 195 nanoseconds, since it is bounded primarily by interconnect topology rather than TLB microarchitecture. Talvera's R4 launch materials reported a 9 percent improvement in transaction-processing throughput attributable specifically to the TLB and walk-cache changes, isolated using otherwise-identical R3 and R4 test systems.

title: Changelog: R4 Walk-Cache Sizing
text: Corvid R4.1 changelog, March 2024 maintenance release. This point release does not alter walk-cache capacity but corrects a documentation error in the original R4 launch notes, whose corrected sentence now reads: The walk cache grew from 512 to 896 entries per core between generations, while cold walk latency dropped from 42 to 37 cycles thanks to a wider memory-controller interface feeding the walker. Silicon behavior is unaffected, since the physical entry count was correct at tape-out; only the publicly documented number was wrong. Engineers who provisioned staging clusters based on the original figure should re-run the walk-cache reconfiguration checklist to confirm their simulated hit-rate projections still hold against the corrected entry count before promoting any pending configuration change to production.

title: Incident Postmortem: The 2023 Shootdown Storm
text: In August 2023, a Sawtooth R3 deployment running a multi-tenant container orchestration platform experienced a sustained throughput collapse that Talvera's field engineering team eventually traced to a TLB shootdown storm rather than any application-level fault. The deployment ran 64 cores across two sockets, hosting several thousand short-lived containers created and torn down continuously as the orchestration platform rebalanced workloads across the cluster. Each container teardown unmapped a set of pages the container had used, and under the platform's default configuration, every unmap triggered a full shootdown broadcast to all 63 remote cores rather than a narrowed broadcast to only the cores that had actually touched the affected address range. During a period of unusually aggressive rebalancing, teardown rate spiked to roughly 1,800 per second, and the resulting shootdown interrupt volume began to saturate the inter-processor interrupt path shared with ordinary scheduling and I/O completion interrupts. Application throughput fell by 74 percent over roughly eleven minutes before an on-call engineer, noticing an anomalous spike in inter-processor interrupt counters rather than any TLB-specific metric, manually throttled the platform's teardown rate as a stopgap. Investigation confirmed Corvid's shootdown logic itself behaved correctly throughout: every acknowledgment arrived, every invalidation completed, and no stale translation was ever observed. The fault lay entirely in the orchestration platform's decision to broadcast shootdowns unconditionally rather than tracking which cores had actually accessed a given container's mappings, bookkeeping the platform had never implemented because earlier, smaller deployments never generated enough shootdown volume to expose the gap. Talvera's recommended remediation, adopted by the platform team within six weeks, was to maintain a per-mapping access bitmap so teardown could narrow its shootdown target list the same way Talvera's own scheduler already did internally, a change that reduced average shootdown fan-out from 63 remote cores to under 4 in production traffic. A secondary recommendation, still under evaluation, proposed a hardware rate limiter inside Corvid that would coalesce shootdown requests arriving faster than a configurable threshold, trading a small amount of additional invalidation latency for protection against this kind of software-driven storm regardless of which platform triggered it. The postmortem's authors noted the interrupt-saturation signature closely resembled an unrelated network-driver incident from 2021, and recommended future on-call runbooks explicitly list shootdown storms as a candidate cause whenever inter-processor interrupt volume spikes without a corresponding rise in scheduling activity. The incident report, published internally in September 2023, has since become required reading during onboarding for engineers supporting multi-tenant Sawtooth deployments.
